content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K .STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Patrick C. Coughlan 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/Legal 
•• 
322-5660 
322-5901 
April 15, 1982 
Westlake Village City Attorney 
Richards, Watson, Dreyfuss & Gershon 
333 South Hope Street, 38th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 
90071 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Advice Letter 
No. A-82-036 
Dear Mr. Coughlan: 
Statements of Econom ic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your request for written advice regarding 
Westlake Village City Councilmembers Bonnie Klove and Irwin 
Shane. 
In addition to our mutual correspondence and numerous 
telephone conversations, I have met with yourself and repre-
sentatives of the other interested parties in this matter. 
They are: 
Mr. George Kimball, Attorney for Westpark Apartments 
Mr. Terry Dowdall, Attorney for Oak Forest Mobile Estates 
Mr. Raphael Chaikin, Co-Owner of Oak Forest r-lobile Estates 
I have also received a telephonic communication from 
Mr. Henderson, a resident of Westlake Village. 
In addition, 
I personally visited Westlake Village and toured Oak Forest 
Mobile Estates, as well as visited Westpark Apartments. 
I 
have also received numerous written communications from 
Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall, as well as an appraisal of Oak 
Forest submitted to Mr. Chaikin by Real Estate Analysts of 
Newport, Inc. 
I have specifically invited counsel for all 
parties to submit arguments on the applicability of various 
provisions of the Political Reform Act and Commission inter-
pretations thereof. 
All of the materials and data submitted 
have been reviewed by me and considered in the analysis of 
this matter. 
I have also consulted with, and received tech-
nical assistance as to legal interpretations of the California 
Mobilehome Residency Law (infra) from Ronald S. Javor, General 
Counsel, State Department of Housing and Community Development 
(HCD), the agency charged with the administration 
that 
body of law. 


Patrick C. Cough_ 
April 15, 1982 
Page Two 
In addition, in response to Mr. Dowdall's repeated 
urgings I have consulted with an expert in mobilehome appraisals, 
a Principal Appraiser for the State Department of Savings 
and Loan. 
The Department's services in this regard are 
utilized by HCD in its appraisal work. 
You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code 
Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove 
and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the 
proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance for 
the City of Westlake Village. 
FACTS 
1. 
Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers 
in the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles 
County. 
Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a 
vote at the November 4, 1981, election. 
2. 
The City is currently subject to a rent control 
ordinance originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to 
incorporation of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted 
and extended by the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers 
Klove and Shane not participating). 
As extended, that ordinance 
will run through June 30, 1985. 
3. 
Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest 
Mobile Estates (the "Park") in Westlake Village. 
Oak Forest 
has a total of 162 spaces, 160 of which are currently occupied. 
Ms. Klove resides in Space 18, Mr. Shane in Space 160. 
4. 
There is only one mobilehome park in Westlake Village -
Oak Forest Mobile Estates. 
There is only one apartment 
complex in Westlake Village - Westpark Apartments. 
No other 
residential rental units exist which are subject to coverage 
of the existing rent control ordinance (single-family units 
are exempted), or the proposed rent control ordinance (4-
units or less are exempted). 
5. 
The Westlake Village City Council is considering 
adopting an alternative rent control ordinance which would 
differ in certain respects from the existing ordinance. 
The 
material substantive differences are as follows: 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Three 
a) 
b) 
c) 
Duration 
Formula for 
Rent Adjust-
ments 
Vacancy 
Decontrol 
Existing 
Until July 1, 1985, 
with optional re-
view at any time 
Council desires. 
75% of CPI increase 
until 6/30/84, then 
100% of CPI increase. 
Apts. - Decontrol upon 
change in tenants -
then, after adjustment, 
recontrol. 
Mobilehomes 
- same. 
Proposed 
Until July 1, 1987, 
with mandatory annual 
review and required 
findings; optional 
review at any time 
Council desires. 
75% of CPI increase 
or 8% whichever is 
less, until 6/30/87. 
Apts. - Permanent 
decontrol upon vacancy. 
Mobilehomes -
no 
decontrol upon change 
in tenants, except 
if removal of mobile-
home as well, then 
permanent decontrol. 
6. 
The current status of Councilmember Klove's and 
Shane's tenancy in the Park is as follows: 
a) 
Klove's lease extension (putativz)l/ expires 
4/30/82, thereafter, by operation of law,_1 her tenancy 
devolves into a 
m~9th-to-month tenancy, or, again by 
operation of law,-
she can elect to have a 12-month 
lease (or any shorter term she elects) or to negotiate 
with the Park owner for a mutually agreeable longer-term 
lease. 
l/ There is pending litigation by the Park against Los Angeles 
County and 17 space tenants in the Park over whether leases were 
entered into which exempted those spaces from the application of 
the County's original rent control ordinance. 
If exempted, rent 
levels would increase in accordance with the terms of the lease 
(100% of CPI) during the lease term, otherwise the levels would be 
controlled by the ordinance. 
During the pendency of the litigation, 
the ordinance controlled levels have applied. 
Regardless of the 
outcome of the litigation, at the expiration of the term of the 
putative leases, rents will be as if the leases had not existed, 
inasmuch as the rents will revert to the controlled level. 
l/ 
3 
Mobilehome Residency Law, Civil Code Section 798, et seq. 
Id. 


Patrick C. Coughl 
April 15, 1982 
Page Four 
b) 
Shane's lease extension (putative)!/ expires 
6/30/82, thereafter the same provisions apply for Shane 
as above-stated for Klove. 
c) 
Under the current rent control ordinance, the 
rent, upon expiration of these putative leases, will 
revert to the level where it is now, consequently the 
existence or non-existence of the putative leases will 
not affect future rents, regardless of the proposed 
ordinance.~/The issue to be resolved by the pending 
litigg1 ion2 
will only determine the amount of back 
rent,-
if any, owing to the Park from Klove and Shane 
for the 38-month term of the putative lease extension. 
The difference is approximately $50 per month. 
d) 
Klove's rent is currently $234 per month and 
Shane's rent is currently $332 per month (ordinance 
controlled levels). 
e) 
Under the current ordinance, if a mobilehome 
tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves 
in, the rent will be adjustable (upward presumably) if 
the previous tenant is on a month-to-month tenancy or 
at the end of a fixed-term lease. 
The rent would there-
after be controlled. 
However, if the previous tenant 
is in the middle of the term of a fixed-term lease, the 
new tenant would take possession subject to that lease 
and would retain the existing level of rent. 
Upon 
expiration of that lease term, the rent would be con-
trolled (not adjustable) under the current ordinance. 
f) 
Under the proposed ordinance, if a mobilehome 
tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves 
in, the rent would remain controlled whether there is a 
lease or not and it would become adjustable only if the 
mobilehome were removed from the space and a new one 
brought in. 
i/ See footnote 1. 
2/ Because the (putative) lease extension periods will ex-
pire before the end of the litigation and because, upon expiration, 
the rents will revert to the current levels as controlled by the 
current ordinance, the only matter to be resolved by the litigation 
is whether the 17 space tenants owe back rent for their respective 
(putative) lease periods. 
The proposed ordinance will have no 
ef 
ct on the litigation nor will the litigation have any effect 
on the proposed ordinance. 
~/ Id. 


Patrick C. Cough 1 
April 15, 1982 
Page Five 
g) 
Park tenants, including Klove and Shane, are 
required to install certain improvements and landscaping 
upon moving onto a space in the Park. 
These improvements 
include a carport. 
Approximate cost of these improvements 
averages $6,500. 
In addition, many tenants have also 
installed customized decking and other improvements. 
Legally, these improvements are not fixtures and remain 
the personal property of the tenants and may be removed 
by the tenants. 
h) 
The mobilehomes in the Park are all of sufficient 
age so as to be registered through the Department of 
Housing and Community Development and display license 
plates~ they are subject to the annual mobilehome tax 
(formerly vehicle use tax). 
None are affixed to permanent 
foundations. 
i) 
The owners of the Park have prepared and submitted 
a study comparing the "market rents" and the current 
rents in the Park. 
The owners stipulate that even if 
there were no rent control in Westlake Village, the 
Park would charge rents below those stated as "market" 
rate rents in the study. 
j) 
The Park owners (and Mr. Kimball) submit that 
the difference between the current rents and the "market 
rents," as shown by their study, results in a "premium" 
in favor of the Park tenants and that this "premium" 
can be capitalized and sold by a Park tenant when selling 
his/her mobilehome (when moving out but leaving the 
coach in place). 
Klove and Shane do not accede to this 
contention. 
However, you do stipulate on their behalf, 
that if such a premium does exist and can result in a 
higher price for sale of their mobilehomes, then that 
differential benefit would correlate with the relati~e 
size of the "premium," all other things being equal.~/ 
7. 
The City of Westlake Village has a population of 
11,500 residents contained in 2,370 housing units which 
consist of 162 mobilehome park spaces, 234 apartments and 
the balance in single-family detached dwellings or condominiums. 
Thus there are basically 400 rental units in Westlake Village 
2/ 
My conversation with Mr. Patrick Connolly, Principal 
Appraiser of the State Department of Savings and Loan, indicates 
that capitalization is totally dependent upon the amount of the 
"premium" and the expectancy of its duration. 
Mr. Dowdall argues 
for a 25-year capitalization. 
However, based upon all of the 
uncertainties involved here, including annual reviews, the extant 
notice of change of use, etc., Mr. Connolly's expert opinion was 
that little more than one-year's worth of any such "premium" could 
be capitalized. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Six 
subject to either of the two ordinances. 
The total residents 
of these two rental complexes is approximately 200 in the 
apartment complex and 320 in the Park. 
8. 
Westpark Apartments is considering possible condominium 
conversion and consequently 100 apartments are currently 
vacant and being held off the market. 
9. 
The owners of the Park have given the required 12-
month notice to Park residents that they may wish to effectuate 
a change of use in the property, possibly to condominiums. 
This notice was given "several years ago." 
10. 
Westlake Village is an upper middle income community. 
11. 
Councilmembers Klove and Shane have stated that 
they could afford to remain residents of the Park if rents 
were increased to the "market rents" in the study, but that 
for tax reasons (the lack of any tax deduction on such high 
rents) and the lack of any equity accumulation (or appreciation), 
they might consider moving if rents did increase to that 
level. 
However, they have no present intention to sell 
their mobilehomes and move. 
ANALYSIS 
T~e Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the 
"PRA")_I mandates that no public official may make or partici-
pate ~n the making of any government decision in which he or 
~he knows 9?r has reason to know that he or she has a financial 
lnterest.-
Financial Interest 
Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a 
financial interest within the meaning of the Act. 
It states 
as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
~I Government Code Sections 81000-91014; hereinafter 
all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 
21 
Section 87100. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Seven 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without regard 
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars 
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior 
to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or 
holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business 
entity or trust in which the official, the official's 
agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, 
indirectly or beneficially a 10 percent interest or 
greater. 
Subdivisions (a) and (d) do not apply to the present 
facts. 
Our focus must, therefore, turn to subdivisions (b) 
and (c). 
A. 
Do Klove and Shane Have An Interest In Real Property 
An interest in real property, as used in the PRA, is 
defined in Section 82033 as follows: 
"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, 
beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire 
such an interest in real property located in the juris-
diction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by 
the public official, or other filer, or his or her 
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest 
is greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000), provided 


Patrick C. Coughl 
April 15, 1982 
Page Eight 
that a leasehold interest does not include a lessee's 
interest in a lease on real property which expires 
within 10 years of the first day of the period covered 
by the filer's statement of economic interest. 
Interests 
in real property of an individual includes a pro rata 
share of interests in real property of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate 
family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 
percent interest or greater. 
1. 
Leasehold 
A residential tenancy (month-to-month or short-term 
lease) has been interpreted by the Commission as constituting 
an interest in real property on the part of the tenant for 
disqualification purposes if certain factors are present. 
Based upon the facts here, we conclude that Klove's and 
Shane's tenancies d018?nstitute interests in real property 
in excess of $1,000.--
2. 
Mobilehome Ownership 
At our meeting, Mr. Chaikin, one of the Park owners, 
stated that all of the improvements installed by the Park 
tenants are removable, including decking and carports. 
As 
previously stated, none of the coaches are affixed to permanent 
foundations. 
Mr. Chaikin further stated that two or three 
tenants had moved out in the past, taking everything with 
them and leaving the "pad" as they originally found it. 
This is confirmed by the terms of the written rental a0reement 
(putative lease) in effect, which, under Part XIII "Termination" 
states, in part, as follows: 
~/ Opinion requested by Martin Overstreet, 6 FPPC 
Opinions 12, 16-17, No. 80-010, March 2, 1981. 
In Overstreet, 
the Commission held that a tenant with a month-to-month 
tenancy permitting only "good-cause" eviction had an interest 
in real property worth more than $1,000 for purposes of 
disqualification, as opposed to disclosure. 
In the instant 
case, both Klove and Shane have tenancies subject to the 
Mobilehome Residency Law (see footnote 2, supra) and, therefore, 
are entitled to certain "good-cause" eviction protections. 
Thus regardless of whether or not the putative-leases are 
legally effective, both Klove and Shane may have an interest 
in real property in excess of $1,000 in their respective 
leaseholds. 
However, for Shane, the term remaining on his 
lease means that this period multiplied by his rent exceeds 
$1,000. 
For Klove, however, because of the required further 
notices, we conclude that her expectation of time remaining, 
when multiplied by the rent, will also exceed $1,000. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Nine 
Upon termination of this lease for any reason, 
lessee agrees, at lessee's sole cost and expense, to 
remove the mobile home, associated structures and any 
improvements placed on the premises by lessee within 
fourteen (14) days of such termination. 
If lessee 
shall not remove the mobile home, associated structures 
and improvements within such time period, lessor shall 
have them moved and charge lessee for the cost of such 
mov ing .... 
California law is clear that personal property is not 
real property. 
(See Civil Code Sections 657 through 663, 
inclusive. 
Cf. Kolstad v. Ghidotty (1963) 212 Cal. App. 2d 
228; Estus v. Weber (1946) 76 Cal. App. 2d 724.) 
The intent 
of the parties is controlling in most instances. 
Cf. Alderman 
v. Baggett (1933) 134 Cal. App. 501. 
In this case-,-the 
intent of the parties is very clear, the mobilehomes and 
other landscaping improvements (decking, carports, etc.) are 
removable and, hence, personal property. 
This conclusion is 
further bolstered by the law in this state surrounding mobile-
homes and their sale. 
'~obilehome" is a structure designed for human 
habitation and for being moved on a street or highway 
under permit pursuant to Section 35790 of the Vehicle 
code. 
Civil Code Section 798.3. 
(See also, Health and Safety 
Code Section 18008.) 
Mobilehomes are sold by licensed mobilehome dealers 
(Health and Safety Code Section 18002.6), who may not sell 
interests in real property unless they are also licensed 
real estate brokers (or agents). 
Business and Professions 
Code Sections 10130-10131.7, inclusive. 
Mobilehomes become real property and are taxed as such 
only if they are installed on a permanent foundation pursuant 
to Health and Safety Code Section 18551. 
Once installed on a foundation system in compliance 
with the provisions of subdivisions (a) and (b), a 
mobilehome shall be deemed a fixture and a real property 
improvement to the real property to which it is affixed. 
Physical removal of the mobilehome shall thereafter be 
prohibited without the consent of all persons or entities 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Ten 
who, at the time of such removal, have title to any 
estate or interest in the real property to which the 
mobilehome is affixed. 
Health and Safety Code Section 
18551(c). 
Such is not the case here and, consequently, the mobilehomes 
in question here may not be taxed as real property. 
Revenue 
and Taxation Code Sections 5801(b) and 5810i Health and 
Safety Code Section 18105.5. 
Therefore, we conclude that Klove's and Shane's mobilehomes 
and other improvements do not constitute an interest in real 
property under Section 82033 of the Act and do not constitute a 
financial interest within the meaning of Sections 87100 and 
87103(b) of the Act. 
Consequently, no conflict of interest 
determination may be predicated upon their ownership of 
their mobilehQmes and the other improvements. 
Summary 
The only interest in real property which Klove and 
Shane have is in their leaseholds. 
B. 
Source of Income 
The Commission, through its regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18702 and through its advice letters, in certain 
circumstances, considers an individual to be a source of 
income to himself as will be discussed in greater detail 
infra. 
Material Financial Effect 
We must next examine the reasonably foreseeable effects 
of the proposed ordinance upon Klove's and Shane's financial 
interests. 
In the case of an interest in real property, this term 
has been devined as follows: 
(a) 
The financial effect of a governemntal decision 
on a financial interest of a public official is material 
if the decision will have a significant effect on the 
business entity, real property or source of income in 
question. 


Patrick C. Cough 1 
April 15, 1982 
Page Eleven 
(b) 
In determining whether it is rea~onably foreseeable 
that the effects of a governmental decision will be 
significant within the meaning of the general standard 
set forth in paragraph (a), consideration should be 
given to the following factors: 
* * * 
(2) 
Whether, in the case of a direct or indirect 
interest in real property of one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more held by a public official, the effect of the 
decision will be to increase or decrease: 
(A) 
The income producing potential of the property 
by the lesser of: 
1. 
One thousand dollars ($1,000) per month; or 
2. 
Five percent per month if it is fifty dollars ($50) 
or more per month; or 
(B) 
The fair market value of the property by the 
lesser of: 
1. 
Ten thousand dollars (10,000); or 
2. 
One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars 
($1,000) or more. 
Generally, the effect upon the value of their tenancies 
would have to be at least $1,000 to be material. 
A. 
Effect of Rent Increase Limitations 
The present rent control ordinance governs Klove's and 
Shane's rents. 
Regardless of the putative leases, when 
those have expired in the near future (if they were ever in 
effect at all), the rents Klove and Shane respectively pay 
will be the same as they currently are and will be adjusted 
in accordance with the formula in the current ordinance 
through June 30, 1985. 
For the next two years there is 
little or no difference as to which ordinance is in effect. 
If inflation (increase in CPI) remains below 10%, then the 
8% cap in the proposed ordinance will not have any effect at 
all. 
Beginning July 1, 1984, the current ordinance would 
permit rents to rise by the full CPI for a specified period, 
whereas the proposed ordinance would continue the 75% (of 
CPI) limit with the 8% cap. 


Patrick C. Coughl 
April 15, 1982 
Page Twelve 
Dependent upon the CPI, there could be a difference in 
the amount of rent which !±?ve and Shane would have to pay 
under the two ordinances.--
However, predictions about the 
CPI are extremely speculative. 
All of this makes any financial 
effect which would be deemed t~/be material so remote as not 
to be reasonably foreseeable.--
Consequently, the proposed 
rent control ordinance creates no foreseeable conflict of 
interest on the part of Councilmembers Klove and Shane as to 
their tenancy interest in real property. 
B. 
Effect of Vacancy Decontrol Provisions 
1. 
Leasehold Interest 
In addition, the proposed ordinance would eliminate 
vacancy decontrol as to mobilehome park tenancies. 
Messrs. 
Dowdall and Kimba±~/argue that this will create a "premium" 
leasehold tenancy--
which the Park tenar~/could then sell 
for a "premium" price which, they argue,--
would constitute 
a "material financial effect" upon a tenant who enjoys such 
a benefit. 
This is a novel approach which does not find any 
support in prior Commission advice, opinions or in our regu-
lations. 
In addition, even if such a theory were adopted by 
the Commission, neither Klove nor Shane will have a leasehold 
in excess of one year in the foreseeable future (unless the 
Park owners agree to a longer term - which is a factor under 
their control). 
Upon expiration of any such lease term, the 
rent would be controlled as to the tenant then in possession. 
Consequently, any benefits to be potentially derived 
from elimination of vacancy decontrol can be achieved currently 
11/ 
Even if the CPI increase for the relevant period 
was 15% (making the difference between the two ordinances 7% 
for the 7/1/84 through 7/30/85 period) the total difference 
in monthly rental rates would be approximately $30 per month, 
an increase of less than $1,000 in a year. 
12/ See Advice Letter to Thomas Toomey, San Francisco 
City Attorney, p.5, June 12, 1981, No. A-81-137 (copy enclosed). 
13/ 
By operation of the rent control ordinance in 
conjunction with the Mobilehome Residency Law (see footnote 
2) the tenancy is arguably a "life estate" in favor of the 
tenant dependent upon the tenant's good behavior. 
14/ See letter of Terry Dowdall, dated March 29, 1982, 
atpp.2-3. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Thirteen 
if Klove and Shane merely exercise their respective statutory 
options to renew on a one-year lease and then sell their 
mobilehomes (and arguably their leases) in mid-term. 
Currently, assignment is limited by the owner's approval. 
However, Park management has indicated that future lease 
extensions (whether for one year, or more, or less) will 
contain a provision terminating the lease at less than full 
term if the tenant sells and moves (but leaves the coach) 
prior to expiration of the lease. 
In that event, then there 
would be no lease to sell. 
We are then faced with the question of whether or not a5 
mobilehome park tenant can "sell" a month-to-month tenancy.~1 
Messrs. Dowdall and Kimball argue that under the MRL a month-
to-month tenancy in a mobilehome park is, in reality, a 16 
"life estate," subject only to a tenant's good behavior-I 17 
and the continued use of the property as a mobilehome park.--I 
However, we cannot conclude that Klove's and Shane's 
month-to-month tenancies may be "sold" for any value which 
might be considered to be a material financial effect. 
Such 
a result is contrary to law, and such valuation of a future 
tenancy was not contemplated by Overstreet. 
2. 
Source of Income Interests 
It is also argued that both Klove and Shane can sell 
their mobilehomes (and reap a "premium" price therefor) if 
vacancy decontrol is eliminated by adoption of the proposed 
ordinance. 
Since their mobilehomes do not represent an 
interest in real property (as discussed previously) the mere 
151 
Civil Code Sections 798.74 and 798.75 make any 
transfer of ownership subject to the prior approval of the 
park owner and require that the incoming purchaser execute a 
rental agreement before the purchaser shall have any rights 
of tenancy. 
A 1981 amendment to Section 798.75 states: 
"In 
the event the purchaser fails to execute the rental agreement, 
the purchaser shall not have any rights of tenancy." 
161 
Civil Code Section 798.56 sets forth certain "good-
cause" reasons for which a tenancy may be terminated. 
171 
Our research indicates that a "life estate," as 
the term is traditionally used in real property law, is not 
what a mobilehome park tenant may enjoy. 
In Overstreet the 
Commission limited consideration of the tenancy as an interest 
in the real property to the period of the occupancy of the 
existing tenant, not to future tenants. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Fourteen 
fact that their value may be increased does not constitute 
an effect upon a financial interest. 
This conclusion is not changed by the recently adopted 
amendment to the Commission's regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18702 which includes within the meaning of "material 
financial effect": 
The effect of the decision will be to directly 
increase or decrease the amount of income (other than 
rents) to be received by the official, or to confer a 
financial benefit or detriment upon the official or a 
member of the official's immediate family, in an amount 
of one hundred dollars ($100) or more. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3) (A). 
While the final adoption of this amendment is not yet 
complete, it merely codifies long-standing Commission policy 
to treat a public official as a source of income to himself/herself 
and to consider as material certain financial consequences 
to the official which flow directly from an official decision. 
Thus in our recent letter to James Rozek, also involving a 
mobilehome park, we stated as follows: 
We have interpreted Section 87l03(c), an effect on 
a source of income, to include a direct financial effect 
on the official, i.e., where a public official will 
either avoid or incur out of pocket payments as a direct 
result of a governmental decision. 
In this situation, 
the decision on whether to continue the operation of 
the Park will directly affect whether Mr. Warner [a 
Park tenant] incurs or avoids relocation expenses. 
Under Commission guidelines, a $100 effect on the offi-
cial is generally considered to be material. 
See 2 
Cal. Adm. Code Section l8702(b)(3) (A)-(D). 
Our under-
standing is that the relocation costs for a mobilehome 
could well exceed $1,000. 
Thus Board decisions on 
whether the Park will be operated would have a material 
financial effect on Mr. Warner, and he would be prohibited 
from participating; as to other Board decisions on the 
Park, Mr. Warner would not have to disqualify himself. 
Advice Letter to James Rozek 
A-81-503, January 28, 1982, at 
p.3. 
(Copy enclosed.) 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Fifteen 
However, no such direct effect will foreseeably flow 
from a decision on the proposed ordinance. 
In the case of Mr. Warner, in the Rozek letter, the 
direct effect of the decision could be to terminate his 
tenancy, thereby necessitating moving expenses. 
In another 
context, we considered the payment, by a fire district, of 
the costs for plowing fire breaks on land owned by the fire 
district board members to constitute a material financial 
effect - again, this was a direct effect. 
In yet another 
context, we considered the payment of fees by the school 
district for publication of candidates' statements for school 
board members to be such a direct effect. 
Here the financial effect which is alleged by Messrs. 
Kimball and Dowdall is an effect upon the market value of 
Klove's and Shane's coaches. 
It is an appreciation (on 
paper) in the value of their personalty. 
This possible financial effect is speculative at best. 
The alleged financial effect is dependent upon Klove or 
Shane deciding to sell and then securing a willing buyer who 
will pay them a premium price for their mobilehome based 
upon no vacancy decontrol for a place in the Park (which may 
change its use or its rules on fairly short notice). 
Klove 
and Shane have indicated that they do not plan to sell their 
mobilehomes and move. 
The hypothesis is also, by its own 
design, dependent upon rent control decisions relative to 
neighboring jurisdictions. 
Therefore, we must conclude that 
the direct effect test is not met and there is no conflict. 
Having concluded that the facts presented here demonstrate 
no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a 
financial interest of Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane, we need not 
address the "public generally" issues otherwise raised by 
this question. 
CONCLUSION 
So long as any proposed rent control ordinance under 
consideration does not !~Jnificantly alter the rents to be 
paid by Klove and Shane--
there can be no conflict. 
If 
~/ For instance, if a proposal was considered which 
would roll back Klove's and Shane's rents to $100 per month 
permanently, then this would have a material financial ef 
ct 
on their financial interest. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Sixteen 
such an ordinaJce were under consideration, then we would 
invite further advice to consider whether a conflict might 
then exist. 
If you have any questions, I may be reached at (916) 
322 -5 901.

sections.facts:
1. 
Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers 
in the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles 
County. 
Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a 
vote at the November 4, 1981, election. 
2. 
The City is currently subject to a rent control 
ordinance originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to 
incorporation of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted 
and extended by the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers 
Klove and Shane not participating). 
As extended, that ordinance 
will run through June 30, 1985. 
3. 
Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest 
Mobile Estates (the "Park") in Westlake Village. 
Oak Forest 
has a total of 162 spaces, 160 of which are currently occupied. 
Ms. Klove resides in Space 18, Mr. Shane in Space 160. 
4. 
There is only one mobilehome park in Westlake Village -
Oak Forest Mobile Estates. 
There is only one apartment 
complex in Westlake Village - Westpark Apartments. 
No other 
residential rental units exist which are subject to coverage 
of the existing rent control ordinance (single-family units 
are exempted), or the proposed rent control ordinance (4-
units or less are exempted). 
5. 
The Westlake Village City Council is considering 
adopting an alternative rent control ordinance which would 
differ in certain respects from the existing ordinance. 
The 
material substantive differences are as follows: 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Three 
a) 
b) 
c) 
Duration 
Formula for 
Rent Adjust-
ments 
Vacancy 
Decontrol 
Existing 
Until July 1, 1985, 
with optional re-
view at any time 
Council desires. 
75% of CPI increase 
until 6/30/84, then 
100% of CPI increase. 
Apts. - Decontrol upon 
change in tenants -
then, after adjustment, 
recontrol. 
Mobilehomes 
- same. 
Proposed 
Until July 1, 1987, 
with mandatory annual 
review and required 
findings; optional 
review at any time 
Council desires. 
75% of CPI increase 
or 8% whichever is 
less, until 6/30/87. 
Apts. - Permanent 
decontrol upon vacancy. 
Mobilehomes -
no 
decontrol upon change 
in tenants, except 
if removal of mobile-
home as well, then 
permanent decontrol. 
6. 
The current status of Councilmember Klove's and 
Shane's tenancy in the Park is as follows: 
a) 
Klove's lease extension (putativz)l/ expires 
4/30/82, thereafter, by operation of law,_1 her tenancy 
devolves into a 
m~9th-to-month tenancy, or, again by 
operation of law,-
she can elect to have a 12-month 
lease (or any shorter term she elects) or to negotiate 
with the Park owner for a mutually agreeable longer-term 
lease. 
l/ There is pending litigation by the Park against Los Angeles 
County and 17 space tenants in the Park over whether leases were 
entered into which exempted those spaces from the application of 
the County's original rent control ordinance. 
If exempted, rent 
levels would increase in accordance with the terms of the lease 
(100% of CPI) during the lease term, otherwise the levels would be 
controlled by the ordinance. 
During the pendency of the litigation, 
the ordinance controlled levels have applied. 
Regardless of the 
outcome of the litigation, at the expiration of the term of the 
putative leases, rents will be as if the leases had not existed, 
inasmuch as the rents will revert to the controlled level. 
l/ 
3 
Mobilehome Residency Law, Civil Code Section 798, et seq. 
Id. 


Patrick C. Coughl 
April 15, 1982 
Page Four 
b) 
Shane's lease extension (putative)!/ expires 
6/30/82, thereafter the same provisions apply for Shane 
as above-stated for Klove. 
c) 
Under the current rent control ordinance, the 
rent, upon expiration of these putative leases, will 
revert to the level where it is now, consequently the 
existence or non-existence of the putative leases will 
not affect future rents, regardless of the proposed 
ordinance.~/The issue to be resolved by the pending 
litigg1 ion2 
will only determine the amount of back 
rent,-
if any, owing to the Park from Klove and Shane 
for the 38-month term of the putative lease extension. 
The difference is approximately $50 per month. 
d) 
Klove's rent is currently $234 per month and 
Shane's rent is currently $332 per month (ordinance 
controlled levels). 
e) 
Under the current ordinance, if a mobilehome 
tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves 
in, the rent will be adjustable (upward presumably) if 
the previous tenant is on a month-to-month tenancy or 
at the end of a fixed-term lease. 
The rent would there-
after be controlled. 
However, if the previous tenant 
is in the middle of the term of a fixed-term lease, the 
new tenant would take possession subject to that lease 
and would retain the existing level of rent. 
Upon 
expiration of that lease term, the rent would be con-
trolled (not adjustable) under the current ordinance. 
f) 
Under the proposed ordinance, if a mobilehome 
tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves 
in, the rent would remain controlled whether there is a 
lease or not and it would become adjustable only if the 
mobilehome were removed from the space and a new one 
brought in. 
i/ See footnote 1. 
2/ Because the (putative) lease extension periods will ex-
pire before the end of the litigation and because, upon expiration, 
the rents will revert to the current levels as controlled by the 
current ordinance, the only matter to be resolved by the litigation 
is whether the 17 space tenants owe back rent for their respective 
(putative) lease periods. 
The proposed ordinance will have no 
ef 
ct on the litigation nor will the litigation have any effect 
on the proposed ordinance. 
~/ Id. 


Patrick C. Cough 1 
April 15, 1982 
Page Five 
g) 
Park tenants, including Klove and Shane, are 
required to install certain improvements and landscaping 
upon moving onto a space in the Park. 
These improvements 
include a carport. 
Approximate cost of these improvements 
averages $6,500. 
In addition, many tenants have also 
installed customized decking and other improvements. 
Legally, these improvements are not fixtures and remain 
the personal property of the tenants and may be removed 
by the tenants. 
h) 
The mobilehomes in the Park are all of sufficient 
age so as to be registered through the Department of 
Housing and Community Development and display license 
plates~ they are subject to the annual mobilehome tax 
(formerly vehicle use tax). 
None are affixed to permanent 
foundations. 
i) 
The owners of the Park have prepared and submitted 
a study comparing the "market rents" and the current 
rents in the Park. 
The owners stipulate that even if 
there were no rent control in Westlake Village, the 
Park would charge rents below those stated as "market" 
rate rents in the study. 
j) 
The Park owners (and Mr. Kimball) submit that 
the difference between the current rents and the "market 
rents," as shown by their study, results in a "premium" 
in favor of the Park tenants and that this "premium" 
can be capitalized and sold by a Park tenant when selling 
his/her mobilehome (when moving out but leaving the 
coach in place). 
Klove and Shane do not accede to this 
contention. 
However, you do stipulate on their behalf, 
that if such a premium does exist and can result in a 
higher price for sale of their mobilehomes, then that 
differential benefit would correlate with the relati~e 
size of the "premium," all other things being equal.~/ 
7. 
The City of Westlake Village has a population of 
11,500 residents contained in 2,370 housing units which 
consist of 162 mobilehome park spaces, 234 apartments and 
the balance in single-family detached dwellings or condominiums. 
Thus there are basically 400 rental units in Westlake Village 
2/ 
My conversation with Mr. Patrick Connolly, Principal 
Appraiser of the State Department of Savings and Loan, indicates 
that capitalization is totally dependent upon the amount of the 
"premium" and the expectancy of its duration. 
Mr. Dowdall argues 
for a 25-year capitalization. 
However, based upon all of the 
uncertainties involved here, including annual reviews, the extant 
notice of change of use, etc., Mr. Connolly's expert opinion was 
that little more than one-year's worth of any such "premium" could 
be capitalized. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Six 
subject to either of the two ordinances. 
The total residents 
of these two rental complexes is approximately 200 in the 
apartment complex and 320 in the Park. 
8. 
Westpark Apartments is considering possible condominium 
conversion and consequently 100 apartments are currently 
vacant and being held off the market. 
9. 
The owners of the Park have given the required 12-
month notice to Park residents that they may wish to effectuate 
a change of use in the property, possibly to condominiums. 
This notice was given "several years ago." 
10. 
Westlake Village is an upper middle income community. 
11. 
Councilmembers Klove and Shane have stated that 
they could afford to remain residents of the Park if rents 
were increased to the "market rents" in the study, but that 
for tax reasons (the lack of any tax deduction on such high 
rents) and the lack of any equity accumulation (or appreciation), 
they might consider moving if rents did increase to that 
level. 
However, they have no present intention to sell 
their mobilehomes and move.
sections.analysis:
T~e Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the 
"PRA")_I mandates that no public official may make or partici-
pate ~n the making of any government decision in which he or 
~he knows 9?r has reason to know that he or she has a financial 
lnterest.-
Financial Interest 
Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a 
financial interest within the meaning of the Act. 
It states 
as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
~I Government Code Sections 81000-91014; hereinafter 
all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 
21 
Section 87100. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Seven 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without regard 
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars 
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior 
to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or 
holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business 
entity or trust in which the official, the official's 
agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, 
indirectly or beneficially a 10 percent interest or 
greater. 
Subdivisions (a) and (d) do not apply to the present 
facts. 
Our focus must, therefore, turn to subdivisions (b) 
and (c). 
A. 
Do Klove and Shane Have An Interest In Real Property 
An interest in real property, as used in the PRA, is 
defined in Section 82033 as follows: 
"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, 
beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire 
such an interest in real property located in the juris-
diction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by 
the public official, or other filer, or his or her 
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest 
is greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000), provided 


Patrick C. Coughl 
April 15, 1982 
Page Eight 
that a leasehold interest does not include a lessee's 
interest in a lease on real property which expires 
within 10 years of the first day of the period covered 
by the filer's statement of economic interest. 
Interests 
in real property of an individual includes a pro rata 
share of interests in real property of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual or immediate 
family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 
percent interest or greater. 
1. 
Leasehold 
A residential tenancy (month-to-month or short-term 
lease) has been interpreted by the Commission as constituting 
an interest in real property on the part of the tenant for 
disqualification purposes if certain factors are present. 
Based upon the facts here, we conclude that Klove's and 
Shane's tenancies d018?nstitute interests in real property 
in excess of $1,000.--
2. 
Mobilehome Ownership 
At our meeting, Mr. Chaikin, one of the Park owners, 
stated that all of the improvements installed by the Park 
tenants are removable, including decking and carports. 
As 
previously stated, none of the coaches are affixed to permanent 
foundations. 
Mr. Chaikin further stated that two or three 
tenants had moved out in the past, taking everything with 
them and leaving the "pad" as they originally found it. 
This is confirmed by the terms of the written rental a0reement 
(putative lease) in effect, which, under Part XIII "Termination" 
states, in part, as follows: 
~/ Opinion requested by Martin Overstreet, 6 FPPC 
Opinions 12, 16-17, No. 80-010, March 2, 1981. 
In Overstreet, 
the Commission held that a tenant with a month-to-month 
tenancy permitting only "good-cause" eviction had an interest 
in real property worth more than $1,000 for purposes of 
disqualification, as opposed to disclosure. 
In the instant 
case, both Klove and Shane have tenancies subject to the 
Mobilehome Residency Law (see footnote 2, supra) and, therefore, 
are entitled to certain "good-cause" eviction protections. 
Thus regardless of whether or not the putative-leases are 
legally effective, both Klove and Shane may have an interest 
in real property in excess of $1,000 in their respective 
leaseholds. 
However, for Shane, the term remaining on his 
lease means that this period multiplied by his rent exceeds 
$1,000. 
For Klove, however, because of the required further 
notices, we conclude that her expectation of time remaining, 
when multiplied by the rent, will also exceed $1,000. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Nine 
Upon termination of this lease for any reason, 
lessee agrees, at lessee's sole cost and expense, to 
remove the mobile home, associated structures and any 
improvements placed on the premises by lessee within 
fourteen (14) days of such termination. 
If lessee 
shall not remove the mobile home, associated structures 
and improvements within such time period, lessor shall 
have them moved and charge lessee for the cost of such 
mov ing .... 
California law is clear that personal property is not 
real property. 
(See Civil Code Sections 657 through 663, 
inclusive. 
Cf. Kolstad v. Ghidotty (1963) 212 Cal. App. 2d 
228; Estus v. Weber (1946) 76 Cal. App. 2d 724.) 
The intent 
of the parties is controlling in most instances. 
Cf. Alderman 
v. Baggett (1933) 134 Cal. App. 501. 
In this case-,-the 
intent of the parties is very clear, the mobilehomes and 
other landscaping improvements (decking, carports, etc.) are 
removable and, hence, personal property. 
This conclusion is 
further bolstered by the law in this state surrounding mobile-
homes and their sale. 
'~obilehome" is a structure designed for human 
habitation and for being moved on a street or highway 
under permit pursuant to Section 35790 of the Vehicle 
code. 
Civil Code Section 798.3. 
(See also, Health and Safety 
Code Section 18008.) 
Mobilehomes are sold by licensed mobilehome dealers 
(Health and Safety Code Section 18002.6), who may not sell 
interests in real property unless they are also licensed 
real estate brokers (or agents). 
Business and Professions 
Code Sections 10130-10131.7, inclusive. 
Mobilehomes become real property and are taxed as such 
only if they are installed on a permanent foundation pursuant 
to Health and Safety Code Section 18551. 
Once installed on a foundation system in compliance 
with the provisions of subdivisions (a) and (b), a 
mobilehome shall be deemed a fixture and a real property 
improvement to the real property to which it is affixed. 
Physical removal of the mobilehome shall thereafter be 
prohibited without the consent of all persons or entities 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Ten 
who, at the time of such removal, have title to any 
estate or interest in the real property to which the 
mobilehome is affixed. 
Health and Safety Code Section 
18551(c). 
Such is not the case here and, consequently, the mobilehomes 
in question here may not be taxed as real property. 
Revenue 
and Taxation Code Sections 5801(b) and 5810i Health and 
Safety Code Section 18105.5. 
Therefore, we conclude that Klove's and Shane's mobilehomes 
and other improvements do not constitute an interest in real 
property under Section 82033 of the Act and do not constitute a 
financial interest within the meaning of Sections 87100 and 
87103(b) of the Act. 
Consequently, no conflict of interest 
determination may be predicated upon their ownership of 
their mobilehQmes and the other improvements. 
Summary 
The only interest in real property which Klove and 
Shane have is in their leaseholds. 
B. 
Source of Income 
The Commission, through its regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18702 and through its advice letters, in certain 
circumstances, considers an individual to be a source of 
income to himself as will be discussed in greater detail 
infra. 
Material Financial Effect 
We must next examine the reasonably foreseeable effects 
of the proposed ordinance upon Klove's and Shane's financial 
interests. 
In the case of an interest in real property, this term 
has been devined as follows: 
(a) 
The financial effect of a governemntal decision 
on a financial interest of a public official is material 
if the decision will have a significant effect on the 
business entity, real property or source of income in

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: . 


Patrick C. Cough 1 
April 15, 1982 
Page Eleven 
(b) 
In determining whether it is rea~onably foreseeable 
that the effects of a governmental decision will be 
significant within the meaning of the general standard 
set forth in paragraph (a), consideration should be 
given to the following factors:

CONCLUSION: So long as any proposed rent control ordinance under 
consideration does not !~Jnificantly alter the rents to be 
paid by Klove and Shane--
there can be no conflict. 
If 
~/ For instance, if a proposal was considered which 
would roll back Klove's and Shane's rents to $100 per month 
permanently, then this would have a material financial ef 
ct 
on their financial interest. 


Patrick C. Cough 
April 15, 1982 
Page Sixteen 
such an ordinaJce were under consideration, then we would 
invite further advice to consider whether a conflict might 
then exist.
embedding.first_500_words: P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K .STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance (916) 322-5662 Patrick C. Coughlan Administration •• Executive/Legal •• 322-5660 322-5901 April 15, 1982 Westlake Village City Attorney Richards, Watson, Dreyfuss & Gershon 333 South Hope Street, 38th Floor Los Angeles, CA 90071 Enforcement 322-6441 Re: Advice Letter No. A-82-036 Dear Mr. Coughlan: Statements of Econom ic Interest 322-6444 Thank you for your request for written advice regarding Westlake Village City Councilmembers Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane. In addition to our mutual correspondence and numerous telephone conversations, I have met with yourself and repre- sentatives of the other interested parties in this matter. They are: Mr. George Kimball, Attorney for Westpark Apartments Mr. Terry Dowdall, Attorney for Oak Forest Mobile Estates Mr. Raphael Chaikin, Co-Owner of Oak Forest r-lobile Estates I have also received a telephonic communication from Mr. Henderson, a resident of Westlake Village. In addition, I personally visited Westlake Village and toured Oak Forest Mobile Estates, as well as visited Westpark Apartments. I have also received numerous written communications from Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall, as well as an appraisal of Oak Forest submitted to Mr. Chaikin by Real Estate Analysts of Newport, Inc. I have specifically invited counsel for all parties to submit arguments on the applicability of various provisions of the Political Reform Act and Commission inter- pretations thereof. All of the materials and data submitted have been reviewed by me and considered in the analysis of this matter. I have also consulted with, and received tech- nical assistance as to legal interpretations of the California Mobilehome Residency Law (infra) from Ronald S. Javor, General Counsel, State Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD), the agency charged with the administration that body of law. Patrick C. Cough_ April 15, 1982 Page Two In addition, in response to Mr. Dowdall's repeated urgings I have consulted with an expert in mobilehome appraisals, a Principal Appraiser for the State Department of Savings and Loan. The Department's services in this regard are utilized by HCD in its appraisal work. You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance for the City of Westlake Village. FACTS 1. Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers in the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles County. Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a vote at the November 4, 1981, election. 2. The City is currently subject to a rent control ordinance originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to incorporation of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted and extended by the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers Klove and Shane not participating). As extended, that ordinance will run through June 30, 1985. 3. Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest Mobile Estates (the "Park") in Westlake Village. Oak Forest has a total of 162 spaces, 160 of which
